After full establishment (2–3 mowings at full height, typically 6–8 weeks post-seeding), a light maintenance fertilizer (0.5 lbs slow-release N/1000 sqft) can begin the regular program.

source: Penn State + Ohio State Extension Cool-Season Establishment
title: Recently Seeded / Establishing Cool-Season Lawn Care
text:
## Watering during germination
- LIGHT AND FREQUENT — brief cycles 2–3 times daily
- Goal: keep the seed bed consistently moist; never let surface dry between cycles
- Each cycle: 5–10 minutes per zone, just enough to wet top 1 inch
- **DO NOT recommend deep infrequent irrigation** during germination — allows surface to dry, kills germinating seed
- The "before 10 AM only" rule for established lawns DOES NOT apply to new seedings — multiple daily cycles including midday are essential

source: Penn State + Ohio State Extension Cool-Season Establishment
title: Recently Seeded / Establishing Cool-Season Lawn Care
text:
## First mowing
- Begin mowing when grass reaches 3–4 inches tall
- Cut to ~2.5–3 inches initially (do not exceed 1/3 rule)
- Make sure mower blades are SHARP — dull blades tear young seedlings
- Soil should be firm (not soft/muddy) before first mow — wait for dry surface

source: Penn State + Ohio State Extension Cool-Season Establishment
title: Recently Seeded / Establishing Cool-Season Lawn Care
text:
## Disease monitoring on new seedings
Damping-off is the primary disease concern, but management differs by temperature:

**At soil temps below 75°F (typical fall overseeding 55–70°F):**
- Damping-off risk is generally LOW
- DO NOT cite Pythium blight or Pythium aphanidermatum — these are warm-season pathogens (require >85–90°F soil temp)
- If mentioning disease at all: "cool-season Pythium damping-off monitoring only if conditions stay persistently wet"
- Preventive fungicide is NOT standard university extension guidance for fall overseedings at moderate temps

**At soil temps above 75°F (late summer / hot conditions):**
- Pythium blight risk increases on new seedlings in wet conditions
- Mefenoxam (Subdue MAXX, Hi-Yield Turf Fungicide) is the appropriate Pythium treatment if damping-off symptoms appear
- Rhizoctonia damping-off treated with azoxystrobin or PCNB — different pathogens, different treatments

**DO NOT recommend preventive fungicide for damping-off** unless:
- Humidity is elevated (>70%)
- Temperatures are warm (>70°F)
- Conditions warrant it (recent rainfall, persistent moisture)

source: Penn State + Ohio State Extension Cool-Season Establishment
title: Recently Seeded / Establishing Cool-Season Lawn Care
text:
## Pythium species accuracy
- **Pythium aphanidermatum** (primary Pythium blight pathogen) is WARM-SEASON — requires air/soil temps >85–90°F
- DO NOT cite Pythium aphanidermatum as a fall overseeding risk at moderate temperatures (55–75°F)
- At fall temperatures, cool-season Pythium species (P. ultimum, P. irregulare) can cause damping-off but are far less virulent
- Use accurate species terminology: "cool-season Pythium damping-off" not "Pythium blight" for fall scenarios

source: Penn State + Ohio State Extension Cool-Season Establishment
title: Recently Seeded / Establishing Cool-Season Lawn Care
text:
## Pre-emergent planning for AFTER establishment
- If planning future pre-emergent applications, ALWAYS include soil temperature trigger
- Example phrasing: "after lawn is established (6–8 weeks post-seed, 2–3 mowings at full height), apply pre-emergent for crabgrass prevention when soil temps reach 50–55°F next spring"
- Don't just say "apply pre-emergent in spring" — be specific about timing AND establishment prerequisites

source: Penn State + Ohio State Extension Cool-Season Establishment
title: Recently Seeded / Establishing Cool-Season Lawn Care
text:
## Establishment timeline reference
| Day | Activity |
|-----|----------|
| 0 | Seed + starter fertilizer (high P) |
| 0–7 | Multiple light irrigations daily; protect from foot traffic |
| 7–14 | Germination visible (varies by species; PRG fastest, KBG slowest) |
| 21–35 | First mowing when grass reaches 3–4 inches |
| 35–60 | Continue mowing; reduce irrigation toward deep-infrequent |
| 60+ | Full establishment; begin regular fertilization program |

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
# Cool-Season Lawn Drought and Summer Stress Management

Cool-season grasses (KBG, tall fescue, fine fescue, perennial ryegrass) experience summer stress in zones 5–7 when temperatures sustain above 85°F or rainfall is deficient.

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
## Two management paths during drought
The homeowner has a clear choice — communicate both options:

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
### Path A: Maintain green turf with irrigation
- Target: **1–1.5 inches TOTAL per week** combining all irrigation and rainfall
- Deliver as 2–3 SESSIONS per week (early morning, before 10 AM)
- Each session 0.3–0.5 inches via cycle-and-soak technique
- NEVER phrase as "1–1.5 inches per session" — that would be 3–4.5 inches/week causing severe overwatering and runoff

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
### Path B: Allow summer dormancy (acceptable extension-endorsed survival strategy)
Per Penn State and OSU: cool-season grasses CAN be allowed to go summer dormant if the homeowner is unable or unwilling to irrigate.
- Reduce irrigation to **0.5 inches every 2–3 weeks** for survival moisture (keeps crown alive without promoting growth)
- Lawn turns straw color but crowns survive
- Resume normal irrigation in fall when daytime highs consistently fall below 85°F
- DO NOT set recovery threshold at 75°F — too conservative; most extension sources recommend 80–85°F resume
- Dormancy is a SURVIVAL strategy, not a failure — frame positively as a valid option

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
## Mowing during drought stress
- **RAISE to MAXIMUM for grass type during drought** — taller grass shades soil, reduces water loss, deeper roots
- Tall fescue: 4 inches
- Kentucky bluegrass: 3.5–4 inches
- Perennial ryegrass: 2.5 inches (do not exceed)
- NEVER lower mowing height during drought — increases water loss and turf damage
- Mow less frequently if growth has slowed (every 2–3 weeks acceptable)

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
## Footprint test
- If footprints remain visible in the lawn after walking on it: water now
- Combined with visual wilting/bluish cast: confirms drought stress
- This trigger applies to both Path A and Path B (irrigate to survival level at minimum)

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
## What NOT to do during drought
- **No fertilization of any kind.** Salt burn risk; stressed turf cannot use nitrogen.
- **No fungicide.** Dry conditions prevent fungal disease development. Soil moisture "dry" + no recent rainfall = no fungal pressure. Foliar fungicide is contraindicated.
  - EXCEPTION: Root/soil diseases (summer patch — Magnaporthe poae, necrotic ring spot — Ophiosphaerella korrae) actually develop UNDER dry heat stress and may warrant a preventive fungicide DRENCH (azoxystrobin or DMI) when heat stress symptoms are present. These are soil-borne, not foliar — distinguish clearly.
- **No weed control / pre-emergent / herbicides** until lawn fully recovers (2–3 weeks of normal growth).
  - Heat-stressed turf cannot tolerate herbicide phytotoxicity
- **No aeration or mechanical disruption** — wounded turf cannot heal during stress.

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
## Cycle-and-soak technique
For drought-stressed or hydrophobic soil:
- 2–3 short cycles per session (10–15 min each)
- Spaced 45–60 minutes apart to allow infiltration
- Goal: 0.3–0.5 inches total per session
- Alternative to one long cycle that runs off the surface

For clay/heavy soils: shorter cycles, longer intervals
For sandy soils: can deliver more per cycle, shorter intervals
Calibrate with catch cups in each zone if precise output is needed.

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
## Hydretain and wetting agents
- Hydretain is a humectant/moisture-retention product. It is NOT a surfactant wetting agent.
- For hydrophobic soil (water beading on surface): penetrant surfactant wetting agents help. Products: Revolution, Aqua-Aid Penetrant, generic penetrant wetting agents labeled for turf.
- **NEVER cite Hydretain irrigation reduction percentages.** Prohibited phrases:
  - "reduces irrigation by [X]%"
  - "up to [X]% reduction"
  - "cuts watering by [X]%"
  - "reduces irrigation frequency by [X]%"
  - Any quantified efficiency claim
- If recommending Hydretain, use ONLY: "may help with moisture retention." Nothing more specific.

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
## High-altitude / high-desert climates
Denver CO, Salt Lake City UT, Albuquerque NM (zones 5b–6a): spring soil temps lag air temps by 3–6 weeks. Drought management considerations:
- Cool nights enable Path B dormancy/recovery cycle
- More efficient water use due to lower humidity but higher evaporation
- Soil moisture monitoring is critical — caliche and clay layers complicate infiltration

source: Penn State + Ohio State + K-State Extension Drought Programs
title: Cool-Season Lawn Drought Management
text:
## Stressgard product clarification
- "Lesco Stressgard" / "Stressgard" is a Syngenta fertilizer/biostimulant line (e.g., 30-0-10 with biostimulants)
- It is NOT a surfactant or wetting agent
- DO NOT recommend Stressgard as a penetrant or wetting agent — that is a different product category
- Stressgard fertilizer is BANNED for centipede (high N) but acceptable for bermuda and other warm-season grasses

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
# Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management

KBG is vulnerable to heat stress and root/crown diseases in summer (July–August in zones 5–7 when daytime temps consistently exceed 85°F).

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
## Summer mowing height
- Raise to **3.5–4 inches** during peak summer heat stress (consistently >85–90°F)
- Taller grass shades soil, reduces water loss, supports deeper roots
- Return to STANDARD 3–3.5 inches as fall temperatures moderate (September onward)
- DO NOT maintain 4 inches into fall — provides no benefit, increases disease pressure

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
## Hot weather irrigation
- Target 1–1.5 inches TOTAL per week (rainfall + irrigation)
- 2–3 sessions per week, early morning (before 10 AM)
- Each session 0.3–0.5 inches via cycle-and-soak
- Deep infrequent encourages root growth; shallow frequent promotes shallow roots and disease

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
## Summer patch (Magnaporthe poae) — primary KBG summer disease
- **Causal pathogen: Magnaporthe poae** — DO NOT call it Magnaporthe nivalis (that is pink snow mold, a completely different cool-weather disease)
- Symptoms: rings or "frogeye" patterns of dead/yellowing turf 1–5 ft diameter
- Soil temperature trigger: 65–80°F at root zone — typical mid-summer
- Favored by drought stress, compacted soil, heavy thatch
- Preventive fungicide: DMI (propiconazole/Banner Maxx) or QoI (azoxystrobin/Scotts DiseaseEx) as SOIL DRENCH (not foliar) — must reach roots
- Apply when soil temps reach 65°F (preventive); curative is far less effective

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
## Necrotic ring spot (Ophiosphaerella korrae)
- Distinct pathogen from summer patch with similar symptoms
- Same temperature triggers, same drench application method
- Both root/crown diseases require DRENCH application, not foliar — fungicide must reach root zone
- Same fungicide options as summer patch

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
## Fungicide note for KBG diseases — DEFAULT: NO FUNGICIDE IN DRY HEAT

**Default behavior in dry heat conditions (humidity <65%, no recent rain): DO NOT recommend any fungicide application.** Discuss disease awareness as monitoring guidance only — "watch for ring/frogeye patterns if conditions stay hot and stress accumulates."

The ONLY exception that permits a fungicide recommendation under dry conditions:
1. The homeowner's profile explicitly describes visible ring or frogeye dead patches consistent with summer patch / necrotic ring spot, AND
2. The recommendation explicitly names "summer patch (Magnaporthe poae)" or "necrotic ring spot (Ophiosphaerella korrae)" in the same sentence as the fungicide, AND
3. The recommendation explicitly specifies "soil drench" application method (not foliar spray), AND
4. The recommendation specifies that irrigation must be applied with the drench to carry the product to the root zone.

If ANY of these conditions are unmet, omit the fungicide recommendation entirely and frame as future monitoring instead.

**Do NOT recommend "preventive fungicide for summer disease pressure" generically — that violates the humidity rule and is not appropriate when no symptoms are visible.**

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
## Product accuracy — Scotts DiseaseEx
- Scotts DiseaseEx Lawn Fungicide contains **AZOXYSTROBIN** (FRAC Group 11 / QoI strobilurin)
- DO NOT call it thiophanate-methyl (FRAC Group 1 / MBC benzimidazole) — that is the active in Scotts' older Disease Control product
- Azoxystrobin and thiophanate-methyl are different classes with different modes of action

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
## Cool weather KBG diseases (for context)
- **Pink snow mold (Microdochium nivale, formerly Fusarium nivale)** — winter/early spring under snow cover. Distinct from summer patch.
- **Gray snow mold (Typhula spp.)** — similar conditions
- **Red thread (Laetisaria fuciformis)** — spring and fall, nitrogen-deficient conditions; usually responds to light N application

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
## Botanical accuracy
- KBG spreads primarily by RHIZOMES (underground horizontal stems that produce new tillers)
- DO NOT say KBG spreads by stolons — stolons are above-ground horizontal runners; KBG does NOT produce stolons
- Bermuda and zoysiagrass have BOTH rhizomes AND stolons
- St. Augustine spreads by above-ground stolons only

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
## Heat-stress hard rules
When current temperature exceeds 85°F sustained:
- DO NOT recommend high-nitrogen fertilizer (heat-stress turf cannot handle quick-release N)
- DO NOT recommend specific high-N product codes (28-0, 32-0, 34-0, 30-0)
- If fertilization mentioned: "defer to fall when temperatures drop below 75°F"
- DO NOT recommend overseeding or seeding — cool-season seed cannot germinate or survive
  - Overseeding should be deferred to fall when SOIL TEMPERATURES drop to 60–65°F (verify with soil thermometer)
- Focus on heat stress management: raise mowing height, deep infrequent irrigation, avoid foot traffic

source: K-State Research and Extension + Purdue Extension
title: Kentucky Bluegrass Summer Heat and Disease Management
text:
## Aeration timing for KBG
- FALL aeration (late August through October) is strongly preferred — KBG is actively recovering with weeks of growing season ahead
- Spring aeration is risky: wounding occurs just before peak summer heat stress
- In high-altitude/high-desert (Denver, SLC, Albuquerque): fall aeration especially preferred (spring soil temps lag air temps)
- NEVER recommend spring aeration for KBG unless homeowner explicitly requested it

source: Purdue Extension E-75-W + Ohio State Extension
title: White Grub Control — Curative vs Preventive
text:
# White Grub Control — Curative vs Preventive Distinction

White grubs (larvae of Japanese beetles, masked chafers, June beetles, European chafers) are the most damaging soil-dwelling turf pests in zones 5–7. Treatment timing depends ENTIRELY on whether the situation is preventive or curative — wrong product for wrong stage = wasted application.

source: Purdue Extension E-75-W + Ohio State Extension
title: White Grub Control — Curative vs Preventive
text:
## Identification — confirm BEFORE treatment
- Cut a 1 sq ft × 2 inch deep flap of turf with a shovel
- Count C-shaped white larvae in the root zone
- Identify by size and raster pattern at base of abdomen
- Common species in Midwest/Northeast: Japanese beetle, masked chafer, European chafer

source: Purdue Extension E-75-W + Ohio State Extension
title: White Grub Control — Curative vs Preventive
text:
## Action threshold for Kentucky bluegrass
- **5–6 grubs per square foot** (many Midwest extension sources including Purdue E-75-W)
- 8–10 grubs/sq ft is on the high end of what's published; using it as the threshold may delay treatment unnecessarily
- For high-value or irrigated turf, the threshold can be lower (3–5/sq ft)
- For drought-stressed turf showing damage: even lower threshold appropriate

source: Purdue Extension E-75-W + Ohio State Extension
title: White Grub Control — Curative vs Preventive
text:
## CURATIVE products (use for ACTIVE grub damage / late-instar grubs already present)
- **Trichlorfon (Dylox)** — fast-acting, contact-and-ingestion. The standard curative product.
  - Apply when grubs are confirmed in top 2–3 inches of soil
  - Water in with 0.5 inch within 24 hours
  - Effective on large/late-instar grubs (3rd instar)
- **Carbaryl (Sevin)** — effective alternative to trichlorfon
  - Same application + water-in requirements

**Use ONLY these for active grub damage.** Do NOT recommend chlorantraniliprole or imidacloprid for active damage — they are preventive products with no curative effect on late-instar grubs.

source: Purdue Extension E-75-W + Ohio State Extension
title: White Grub Control — Curative vs Preventive
text:
## PREVENTIVE products (apply BEFORE egg hatch)
Apply May–July before egg hatch:
- **Chlorantraniliprole (Scotts GrubEx1, GrubEx, Acelepryn)** — PREVENTIVE ONLY
  - Long residual; targets early-instar grubs as they hatch
  - DOES NOT effectively control large or late-instar grubs already present
  - NEVER recommend as a curative treatment for active grub damage
- **Imidacloprid (Bayer Season-Long Grub Control, Merit, GrubEx with imidacloprid)** — PREVENTIVE
  - Similar May–July application window
  - Soil systemic; controls early-instar grubs

source: Purdue Extension E-75-W + Ohio State Extension
title: White Grub Control — Curative vs Preventive
text:
## Sequencing — curative + preventive follow-up
When active grub damage is visible mid-season:
1. **NOW**: Apply trichlorfon or carbaryl to control the active infestation
2. **FOLLOW-UP next May–July**: Apply preventive chlorantraniliprole or imidacloprid before the next generation's egg hatch
3. Continue preventive in subsequent years if grub pressure persists in the area

This sequence avoids the common mistake of recommending GrubEx for active damage — that's just feeding eggs that haven't hatched yet.

source: Purdue Extension E-75-W + Ohio State Extension
title: White Grub Control — Curative vs Preventive
text:
## Recovery overseeding after grub damage
- Wait until grubs are controlled (1–2 weeks after curative treatment) before overseeding bare patches
- Soil temp must support germination (60–65°F for cool-season)
- Damaged areas often need extensive raking to provide seed-to-soil contact
- Apply starter fertilizer at seeding (high-P 12-24-12 or 18-24-12)
- Do NOT apply pre-emergent in damaged/seeded areas — will prevent overseeding
- Fall pre-emergent timing may CONFLICT with new seedling establishment — acknowledge timing tension and prioritize seeding success over pre-emergent for the immediate season

source: Purdue Extension E-75-W + Ohio State Extension
title: White Grub Control — Curative vs Preventive
text:
## Starter fertilizer accuracy
When recommending starter fertilizer for grub-damaged repair areas:
- Use a "high phosphorus" starter formulation: 12-24-12, 18-24-12, or 24-25-4 type products
- DO NOT recommend Scotts 24-25-4 alongside framing as 12-24-12 — these are different N-to-P ratios; be internally consistent
- Apply AT SEEDING (day 0 to day 3 maximum)
- Light irrigation to incorporate, but seed bed must stay moist during germination

source: Cornell + Penn State Extension Drainage and Soil Health
title: Overwatering and Waterlogged Soil Management
text:
# Overwatering and Waterlogged Soil Management

When soil is saturated (wet) and recent rainfall ≥ 2 inches, the lawn's problems are typically anaerobic conditions — NOT fungal disease.

source: Cornell + Penn State Extension Drainage and Soil Health
title: Overwatering and Waterlogged Soil Management
text:
## Misdiagnosis warning
Yellow patches and decline in low wet areas are PRIMARILY caused by **anaerobic soil conditions (oxygen deprivation / root suffocation)** — not fungal disease.

- DO NOT leap to fungicide as the diagnosis for yellow patches in wet soil
- Overwatering symptoms and disease look similar but have different causes
- Apply fungicide only if there is CLEAR evidence of disease: visible lesions, target-shaped patches with distinct margins, dollar-spot-style sunken spots

source: Cornell + Penn State Extension Drainage and Soil Health
title: Overwatering and Waterlogged Soil Management
text:
## Hard rules for waterlogged soil
- **REDUCE irrigation immediately** — let soil dry between events
- **DO NOT recommend fertilizer** until soil moisture normalizes; fertilizer on saturated soil runs off and does not benefit lawn
- DO NOT recommend pre-emergent or post-emergent herbicides — they're carried off in runoff
- DO NOT recommend fungicide unless distinct disease evidence is present

source: Cornell + Penn State Extension Drainage and Soil Health
title: Overwatering and Waterlogged Soil Management
text:
## Priority interventions
1. **Reduce irrigation** — turn off automatic systems; rely on rainfall
2. **Improve drainage**:
   - Core aeration (relieves compaction; opens channels for oxygen)
   - Topdressing low spots with coarse sand to raise grade
   - French drain consideration for chronically wet areas
   - Redirect downspouts away from problem areas
3. **Allow recovery** — anaerobic damage heals once oxygen returns; turf often recovers without further intervention

source: Cornell + Penn State Extension Drainage and Soil Health
title: Overwatering and Waterlogged Soil Management
text:
## Aeration on saturated soil
- DO NOT core aerate when soil is actively saturated (wet)
- Wait for soil to dry to "moist" before aerating (typically 3–5 days after standing water clears)
- Aerating saturated soil creates a muddy mess and doesn't deliver the soil opening benefits

source: Cornell + Penn State Extension Drainage and Soil Health
title: Overwatering and Waterlogged Soil Management
text:
## When fungicide IS warranted in wet conditions
If distinct disease symptoms ARE present alongside wet soil:
- **Pythium blight** (Pythium aphanidermatum) requires warm AND wet (>85°F + saturated soil/leaf wetness). Mefenoxam (Subdue MAXX, Hi-Yield Turf Fungicide) is the appropriate treatment.
- **Brown patch** (Rhizoctonia solani) at 75–85°F + humidity: QoI (azoxystrobin) or DMI (propiconazole)
- **Red thread** (Laetisaria fuciformis) at 60–75°F + nitrogen deficiency: light N application often resolves; fungicide rarely needed

For these distinct diseases, fungicide is appropriate. But the DEFAULT diagnosis for yellow patches in wet soil should be anaerobic damage, not disease.

source: Cornell + Penn State Extension Drainage and Soil Health
title: Overwatering and Waterlogged Soil Management
text:
## Compaction and thatch as contributors
- Compacted soil cannot drain — exacerbates overwatering symptoms
- Thatch >0.5 inches holds water at the surface and creates anaerobic mat
- These structural issues are root causes; addressing them is the long-term fix
- Core aeration twice yearly (spring + fall for cool-season; spring + summer for warm-season) prevents compaction
- Dethatching when thatch exceeds 0.5 inches

source: Cornell + Penn State Extension Drainage and Soil Health
title: Overwatering and Waterlogged Soil Management
text:
## Smart irrigation principles
- Match irrigation to ET (evapotranspiration) loss + rainfall
- Most established lawns need 1–1.5 inches per week TOTAL (cool-season); centipede needs only 0.5–0.75 inches (humid SE); Phoenix bermuda 1–1.5 inches
- Use a rain gauge or smart controller with rainfall sensor to avoid irrigating during/after rain
- The "deep and infrequent" principle: encourage deep roots by watering less frequently but more thoroughly per session
- Cycle-and-soak for clay soils to allow infiltration without runoff

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
# Grass Type Identification Guide (Cool-Season vs Warm-Season)

When grass type is unknown, identification is the FIRST priority — every species has different mowing heights, fertilization needs, disease pressures, and herbicide tolerances.

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
## Initial classification — cool-season vs warm-season
**Where you live tells you what you likely have:**
- Zone 5–7 (Midwest, Northeast): cool-season dominant (KBG, tall fescue, perennial ryegrass, fine fescue)
- Zone 8 transition: mix of both; warm-season more common
- Zone 9–10 (Southeast, Southwest): warm-season dominant (bermuda, zoysia, St. Augustine, centipede, buffalo)

**Seasonal behavior:**
- Cool-season: actively grows in spring and fall; struggles in summer heat (browns or thins); green through fall and into winter in mild zones
- Warm-season: greens up in late spring; thrives in summer; goes DORMANT (brown/straw) when temperatures drop below 50°F sustained

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text: ## Key visual identifiers

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
### Spreading mechanism
- **Stolons only** (above-ground runners): St. Augustine, centipede
- **Rhizomes only** (underground stems): Kentucky bluegrass, tall fescue (limited rhizomes)
- **Both stolons + rhizomes**: bermuda, zoysia
- **Bunch-type** (no spreading runners): perennial ryegrass, fine fescue

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
### Blade width
- **Very fine** (< 2mm): fine fescue, Zoysia matrella (Zeon, Cavalier), bermuda hybrid
- **Fine** (2–3mm): Zoysia japonica (Meyer, Z-52), perennial ryegrass, bermuda common
- **Medium** (3–5mm): Kentucky bluegrass, centipede
- **Wide** (>5mm): St. Augustine (very wide), tall fescue (medium-wide)

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
### Blade tip & shape
- **Boat-shaped** (pointed-cupped): Kentucky bluegrass — distinctive identifier
- **Pointed**: tall fescue, perennial ryegrass
- **Rounded/blunt**: St. Augustine, centipede

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
### Other distinguishing features
- **Tall fescue:** coarse texture, prominent VEINS (not midrib), rolled vernation, bunch-type growth. DO NOT identify tall fescue by "prominent midrib" — that's St. Augustine.
- **Perennial ryegrass:** prominent midrib visible from below, glossy underside of blade, folded vernation
- **St. Augustine:** prominent midrib, very wide blade (5+ mm), rounded tip, stoloniferous spreading runners
- **Kentucky bluegrass:** boat-shaped pointed tip, medium-width blade, rhizomatous (underground spreading)
- **Bermuda:** very fine blade with small ligule hairs (fringe), grayish-green color, dense low-growing

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
### Vernation (how new leaves emerge from sheath)
- **Folded**: perennial ryegrass, KBG, zoysia
- **Rolled**: tall fescue, bermuda, St. Augustine
- (Less useful for homeowner identification; mention if relevant)

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
## Quick identification flow
1. **Is the lawn green right now in winter?** (below 50°F sustained)
   - YES: cool-season grass (KBG, fescue, PRG)
   - NO (brown/dormant): warm-season grass (bermuda, zoysia, SA, centipede, buffalo)
2. **Where is the lawn?**
   - Northern US (Zone 5–7): cool-season
   - Southern US (Zone 8–10): warm-season
3. **What do the blades look like?** Apply blade width + tip + spreading mechanism above
4. **What's the recent care history?** Type of sod laid (if known), prior seeding, common grass for the region

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
## What to do when grass type is unknown
- LEAD with grass type identification guidance in your recommendations
- Suggest: take close-up photos and submit to a local Cooperative Extension office for identification
- Self-identification guide: provide the key visual differences above so homeowner can assess
- Until identified, give GENERIC guidance with explicit uncertainty framing

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
## Generic guidance when type is unknown
- **Mowing height**: 2–3.5 inches as a generic safe range. Caveat that specific range depends on species.
- **Fertilization**: do NOT recommend specific N rates or NPK formulas. Provide only general timing guidance ("warm-season grasses fertilize during active summer growth; cool-season grasses fertilize fall + light spring").
- **Disease + pest**: defer species-specific advice until identified. General monitoring guidance acceptable.
- **Pre-emergent**: frame as general guidance without species-specific timing — soil temperature triggers still apply but optimal product depends on species

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
## Cornell Cooperative Extension resources (Northeast)
- For ZIP codes in NY state (10xxx–14xxx): nearest Cornell Cooperative Extension county office can help with identification
- For NYC specifically (ZIP 10xxx): **CCE New York City — cceny.org** (the correct NYC CCE site). Cornell's CALS extension portal is **harvest.cals.cornell.edu**. AVOID citing "ccenyc.org" (incorrect domain) or "ccetompkins.org" (that is the Tompkins County office in Ithaca, not NYC).
- For soil testing in NY area: Cornell Soil Health Laboratory (Cornell University, Ithaca) or UMass Soil Testing Lab (acceptable cross-state for NYC homeowners). DO NOT recommend Whitetail Institute — that's a food plot brand, not a turf soil test lab.
- Submitting clear photos of a 4×4 inch lawn sample + blade close-up usually allows identification

source: Cornell Cooperative Extension + UMN Extension Turf ID
title: Grass Type Identification Guide
text:
## Bermuda is unlikely in NYC (Zone 7a)
- DO NOT cite bermuda grass as a comparison example for mowing height when discussing NYC lawns — bermuda is extremely unlikely at Zone 7a; could mislead the homeowner about realistic grass type possibilities
- Likely candidates for NYC area: Kentucky bluegrass, tall fescue, fine fescue (cool-season)
